We are given two straight lines AB and BC, which are incommensurable in square. Do you agree? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This is stated in the hypothesis of the proposition.` },
{ q: `We are also told that the sum of the squares on AB and BC is medial. Is that correct? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This is one of the given conditions.` },
{ q: `We are further told that the rectangle contained by AB and BC is medial and incommensurable with the sum of the squares on them. Do you agree? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This completes the given conditions, as in X.35.` },
{ q: `We now add the straight lines AB and BC together to form AC. Is that what is done? (yes/no):`,
hint: `AC is the whole straight line formed from AB and BC.` },
{ q: `Our goal is to prove that AC is irrational, and to name it the side of the sum of two medial areas. Does that make sense? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This is exactly the claim of the proposition.` },

{ q: `Let a rational straight line DE be set out. Can we do this? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Euclid often begins by setting out a rational straight line for comparison.` },
{ q: `To DE, let the rectangle DF be applied equal to the squares on AB and BC together. Is this possible? (yes/no):`,
hint: `A rectangle equal to a given area can be applied to a given line.` },
{ q: `Let the rectangle GH be applied equal to twice the rectangle contained by AB and BC. Can we also do this? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Twice a given rectangle can likewise be constructed.` },
{ q: `Therefore, is the whole rectangle DH equal to the square on AC? (yes/no):`,
hint: `By II.4, (square on AC) = (squares on AB and BC) + 2·(rectangle AB,BC).` },

{ q: `Since the sum of the squares on AB and BC is medial, and DF is equal to that sum, is DF medial? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Equals to the same thing are of the same type.` },
{ q: `Is DF applied to the rational straight line DE? (yes/no):`,
hint: `DF was constructed upon DE.` },
{ q: `Therefore, is DG rational and incommensurable in length with DE? (yes/no):`,
hint: `By X.22, a medial area applied to a rational line produces a rational breadth incommensurable in length with the base.` },
{ q: `For the same reason, is GK also rational and incommensurable in length with GF, that is, with DE? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Applying a medial area to the same rational base yields the same kind of breadth relation.` },

{ q: `Since the squares on AB and BC are incommensurable with twice the rectangle AB, BC, is DF incommensurable with GH? (yes/no):`,
hint: `DF equals the sum of squares, and GH equals twice the rectangle; the given incommensurability transfers by equality.` },
{ q: `Therefore, are DG and GK also incommensurable with one another? (yes/no):`,
hint: `By VI.1 the rectangles on the same base have the ratio of their breadths, and by X.11 incommensurability of areas implies incommensurability of the corresponding sides.` },
{ q: `Are DG and GK nevertheless rational straight lines? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Each arises as the breadth from a medial area applied to a rational base, hence is rational (X.22).` },
{ q: `Since they are rational and commensurable in square only, is DK irrational and called binomial? (yes/no):`,
hint: `By X.36, the sum of two rational straight lines commensurable in square only is an irrational straight line called binomial.` },

{ q: `Is DE rational? (yes/no):`,
hint: `DE was assumed rational at the start.` },
{ q: `Therefore, is DH irrational? (yes/no):`,
hint: `DH is the rectangle on DE with breadth DK; since DK is irrational, the resulting area cannot be rational.` },
{ q: `Is the side of the square equal to DH irrational? (yes/no):`,
hint: `By Definition X.4, if the square is irrational, its side is irrational.` },
{ q: `But is AC the side of the square equal to DH? (yes/no):`,
hint: `DH was constructed equal to the square on AC.` },
{ q: `Therefore, is AC irrational? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This is the required conclusion.` },
{ q: `And is AC rightly called the side of the sum of two medial areas? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Its defining construction uses the sum of two medial squares (and the associated medial rectangle) to form the square on AC.` },
{ q: `Therefore, have we demonstrated the proposition? (yes/no):`,

{ q: `Let the straight line AB be set out and cut into unequal parts at points C and D. Do you agree? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This sets up the lemma.` },
{ q: `Let AC be greater than DB. Is this assumed? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This is given in the lemma.` },
{ q: `Our goal is to show that the squares on AC and CB are greater than the squares on AD and DB. Does that make sense? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This is the lemma’s claim.` },

{ q: `Let AB be bisected at E. Is this possible? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Any straight line can be bisected.` },
{ q: `Since AC is greater than DB, if DC is subtracted from each, is AD greater than CB? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Subtracting equals from unequals preserves inequality.` },
{ q: `Is AE equal to EB? (yes/no):`,
hint: `E is the midpoint.` },
{ q: `Therefore, is DE less than EC? (yes/no):`,
hint: `If AD > CB, then D lies farther from E than C does on the opposite side, so DE < EC.` },
{ q: `So are points C and D not equidistant from E? (yes/no):`,
hint: `This restates the inequality DE ≠ EC.` },

hint: `The same proposition applies.` },
{ q: `Therefore, are these two sums equal to one another? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Things equal to the same thing are equal.` },
{ q: `Of these, is the square on DE less than the square on EC? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Because DE < EC.` },
{ q: `Therefore, is the rectangle AC, CB less than the rectangle AD, DB? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Subtracting the lesser square from equal wholes leaves a lesser remainder.` },
{ q: `Is twice the rectangle AC, CB also less than twice the rectangle AD, DB? (yes/no):`,
hint: `Doubling preserves inequality.` },
{ q: `Therefore, is the sum of the squares on AC and CB greater than the sum of the squares on AD and DB? (yes/no):`,
hint: `By II.5, (AC² + CB²) = 2·(AC·CB) + 2·(EC²) and similarly for AD,DB; since EC² > DE², the first sum of squares is greater.` },
{ q: `Have we proved the lemma? (yes/no):`,
hint: `The desired inequality between the two sums of squares has now been derived.` }
